envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The coherent outsourced-service model is recurring production-contract revenue from an integrator or processor, commonly linked to delivered bird or live-weight performance and sometimes adjusted for mortality, feed conversion, or relative results. Independent spot and product sales exist in the NAICS code but fall outside the fixed service lens.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to contract grow-out establishments because NAICS 112390 combines contract husbandry services with economically different owned-flock product and niche game-bird models; only contract grow-out is a recurring outsourced service under the fixed lens.
lens.excluded_activities: Independent farms whose primary revenue is the sale of owned birds, meat, eggs, breeding stock, or hunting-release birds; backyard and hobby flocks; poultry hatcheries; chicken and turkey producers; integrator-owned captive farms; processors; and ornamental-bird operations.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market contract growers that repeatedly raise non-chicken, non-turkey poultry such as ducks, geese, quail, pheasants, emus, and ostriches for external integrators or processors, providing housing, equipment, utilities, husbandry, biosecurity, records, and labor for a grow-out fee.

a.bridge: BLS task descriptions for farm and ranch animal workers and managers, plus poultry-house monitoring and robotics evidence developed mainly for chickens, are transferred to other-poultry contract grow-out. Feeding, watering, ventilation, and some monitoring are already automated; AI can expose remaining recordkeeping, anomaly detection, flock monitoring, scheduling, reporting, and purchasing tasks, while cleaning, catching, loading, maintenance, and animal handling remain physical. Chicken-house evidence may overstate applicability to smaller or outdoor niche flocks and understate it for intensive duck operations.
caveats: No NAICS 112390 occupation-by-hours dataset was located.
Most precision-poultry research concerns broilers or layers rather than the species in this code.
Technical exposure should not be interpreted as safe removal of human animal-welfare or biosecurity accountability.
a.change_evidence: Time-and-motion data for commercial duck, quail, pheasant, and other-poultry contract farms, split by already-automated, AI-exposed, and irreducibly physical tasks, plus observed adoption results, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Most operating hours are animal care and facility work, much of it physical or already mechanized. The incremental AI opportunity is strongest in sensor interpretation, mortality and welfare detection, environmental optimization, compliance documentation, performance benchmarking, and administrative coordination.

caveats: No unified NAICS 112390 demand series was located.
Food poultry, breeding stock, and hunting-release birds follow different demand cycles.
Highly pathogenic avian influenza can abruptly destroy flocks, halt placements, and restrict trade.
d5.change_evidence: A five- to ten-year U.S. series for duck, goose, quail, pheasant, emu, and ostrich placements, slaughter, real sales, imports, contract grow-out volume, and processor capacity by species would materially change the multiplier.
d5.rationale: The service ultimately depends on niche food, breeding, and game-bird demand across several species, for which no coherent five-year constant-price series was located. Baseline volume is treated as roughly stable: poultry protein has established demand, but other poultry is smaller and more discretionary than chicken, and disease outbreaks, trade restrictions, processor decisions, restaurant demand, and species mix can cause large swings.

caveats: The code spans multiple species and both food and game-bird markets.
The evidence does not measure lower-middle-market status or current other-poultry contract prevalence.
The firm-count dataset input is missing and was not researched or replaced.
e.change_evidence: A current ARMS or Census microdata tabulation for NAICS 112390 showing production-contract status, grower-fee revenue, ownership, normalized EBITDA, integrator concentration, and independent versus captive operation would determine eligibility.
e.rationale: USDA establishes that production contracts are widespread in poultry and historically included other poultry, but it does not isolate the present share of NAICS 112390 lower-middle-market firms that are contract growers. Eligibility is therefore discounted for independent owned-flock sellers, hobby-like operations, very small niche producers, captive integrator facilities, concentrated counterparties, and operations whose normalized earnings or transferability do not fit the lens.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: This is a structural judgment rather than an observed displacement rate.
Automation proven for chickens may not transfer economically to smaller species niches.
Vertical integration can remove the outsourced grower while leaving physical operator work intact.
o.change_evidence: Evidence that integrators are replacing contract farms with autonomous captive facilities, together with measured human-hours and accountability requirements at commercial other-poultry sites, would materially revise the factor.
o.rationale: Raising live birds still requires an accountable operator to maintain housing, utilities, welfare, biosecurity, equipment, records, and emergency response. AI and robotics can reduce monitoring and routine labor but are unlikely to supply husbandry entirely as software within five years. The main displacement route is integrator self-operation or highly automated captive farms, not elimination of physical production.

q.bridge: USDA evidence on broiler production contracts is used to infer value retention for other-poultry contract growers. Broiler growers receive per-pound or performance-adjusted fees, often under short written terms despite long relationships, and integrators provide birds, feed, transport, veterinary support, and technical guidance. Similar bargaining structure would permit integrators to capture part of AI-driven efficiency through tournament comparisons, fee resets, operating mandates, or nonrenewal. The proxy may understate retention for scarce specialty growers with differentiated capabilities and overstate it where a single local integrator dominates.
caveats: Broiler tournament systems may not represent duck, quail, pheasant, goose, or ratite contracts.
Long relationship tenure does not imply durable written pricing protection.
Integrator concentration can vary sharply by geography and species.
q.change_evidence: Current other-poultry contracts showing fee formulas, input responsibility, benchmarking, technology clauses, renewal duration, capital-upgrade mandates, and realized grower margins before and after efficiency improvements would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Contract growers can retain benefits that reduce their own utilities, labor, mortality, or maintenance cost, but performance-linked pay and concentrated buyer power create substantial pass-through risk. Short contract terms allow renewal repricing even when the commercial relationship is long-lived.

caveats: Research prototypes and grant objectives are not adoption measurements.
Small flocks may not support the fixed cost of advanced sensors or robotics.
Integrator control over specifications may slow or accelerate adoption independently of grower economics.
rho.change_evidence: Integrator-approved pilots on other-poultry farms reporting deployment cost, uptime, false-positive rates, labor hours, mortality, feed conversion, welfare outcomes, and contract payments over multiple flocks would materially revise implementation expectations.
rho.rationale: Within the exposed task slice, five-year implementation should be feasible for monitoring, alerts, documentation, and routine planning, but constrained by thin farm IT teams, inconsistent sensor coverage, species-specific model performance, integrator approval, capital budgets, false alarms, and biosecurity and welfare obligations. Emerging caretaker robots indicate potential but not proven broad commercial adoption.

s5.bridge: USDA's all-farm producer demographics are used as a broad succession proxy: 38% of U.S. producers were age 65 or older in 2022 and average age was 58.1. The five-year qualifying-transfer range is far below the age share because age does not equal sale intent, family succession is common, integrator consent and specialized facilities can impede transfer, and other-poultry contract growers may differ from farms overall. This can overstate third-party control transfers if farms remain within families or understate them if debt, disease, or contract pressure accelerates exits.
caveats: The demographic proxy covers all farms, not other-poultry contract growers.
Producer age does not identify sale intent or completed control transfers.
Highly specialized houses and local integrator dependence may reduce marketability.
s5.change_evidence: Species-specific owner age, succession intentions, contract-assignment terms, integrator approval outcomes, broker listings, and completed going-concern transactions for other-poultry farms would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: An aging producer population supports succession pressure, but a transferable contract farm requires viable buildings, biosecurity history, an assignable or replaceable integrator relationship, and acceptable economics. Closure, family transfer, or asset liquidation may occur without a qualifying control sale.

principal_driver: A contract grower provides recurring, physically indispensable husbandry and facility capacity that software alone cannot replace.
principal_weakness: Other-poultry-specific evidence is sparse, the industry code mixes incompatible species and revenue models, and concentrated integrators can appropriate AI savings or eliminate an outsourced grower relationship.
